144.reason: [Error: The loop condition incorrectly checks for "/*" instead of "*/", and does not guard against out-of-bounds access when i+1 equals content.length(), causing potential ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Also, the loop may not properly skip the closing "*/".]

187.reason: Missing boundary check for i+1 causes IndexOutOfBoundsException when '&' or '|' appears at the end of the input; additionally, the code continues to add a token for the single character after flagging the error.

reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 0.5 , reason:  Missing boundary check for i+1 causes IndexOutOfBoundsException when '&' or '|' appears at the end of the input; additionally, the code continues to add a token for the single character after flagging the error.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.25 , reason:  Missing bounds check for i+1 < content.length(), which will cause StringIndexOutOfBoundsException if '&' is at the end of the file.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.25 , reason:  [This condition triggers an error when a single '&' is found, which is correct per error type 'a'. However, it fails to check if i+1 is within bounds (i.e., if '&' is the last character in the file). Accessing content.charAt(i+1) when i == content.length()-1 causes StringIndexOutOfBoundsException, crashing the program instead of reporting error 'a'. This explains the "javac: not found" red herring — the real issue is unhandled index out of bounds during lexical analysis.]

169.reason: [Assumes escape sequence is always 2 characters inside quotes (e.g., '\n'), but substring(i,i+4) takes 4 chars: '\', escape char, and closing ', which is correct only if input is well-formed. However, no bounds check for i+3 < content.length(), leading to potential StringIndexOutOfBoundsException or incorrect parsing if file ends early. More critically, this logic does not validate whether the character constant actually has exactly one character (or valid escape), violating lexical rules. But the immediate bug relevant to error type 'a' is elsewhere.]
